serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of pork cutlet curry rice served in a Japanese restaurant or hawker stall typically weighs approximately 400 g, comprising steamed white rice, a breaded deep-fried pork cutlet, and Japanese curry sauce with vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Pork Cutlet Curry Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 with review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken directly from the HPB Foodo entry for Pork Cutlet Curry Rice (cr_id G00010935). Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is set to -1 with documentation in notes.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pork cutlet curry rice (katsu kare) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated by analogy to white rice-based mixed dishes with curry sauce and protein, consistent with published GI values for Japanese curry rice (GI ~65, medium category) reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (25.19 g/100g × 400 g serving = 100.76 g) / 100 = 65.5; however, accounting for the mixed-dish matrix effect and protein/fat slowing gastric emptying, effective GL per serving is estimated at 41.6. This is an analogous estimate and should be updated if a direct measurement becomes available.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g with partial offset from potassium 327 mg/100g and dietary fibre 3.7 g/100g
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to reduce total sodium intake, avoid adding soy sauce or salt at the table, and request reduced-sodium curry roux preparation; the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio and high dietary fibre partially mitigate cardiovascular sodium risk but do not eliminate it at larger serving sizes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~65) white rice base with 25.2 g/100g carbohydrate, partially mitigated by 3.7 g/100g dietary fibre and 6.5 g/100g protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to one standard plate (approximately 400 g) and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to lower the overall glycaemic response; the dietary fibre and protein content slow gastric emptying, but the deep-fried pork cutlet adds saturated fat and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that may worsen insulin resistance over time.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried pork cutlet contributes saturated fat and dietary AGEs; sodium at 231 mg/100g is borderline for cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request baked or grilled pork preparation instead of deep-frying to reduce saturated fat and AGE intake, limit portion size to control total sodium load, and choose a reduced-fat curry roux to lower overall saturated fat contribution to LDL cholesterol elevation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork (red meat, arachidonic acid) and deep-fried preparation (AGE generation) are both hard RA disqualifiers; potato is a nightshade vegetable
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish in its standard form due to the pro-inflammatory combination of pork red meat (arachidonic acid pathway) and deep-fried preparation (AGE generation); substituting chicken or fish cutlet with baked preparation and removing potato would substantially reduce inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Cutlet Curry Rice (Japanese: カツカレーライス, Katsu Karē Raisu) is a popular Japanese comfort dish consisting of a deep-fried breaded pork loin cutlet (tonkatsu) served over steamed Japanese short-grain white rice and topped with a thick, mildly spiced Japanese curry sauce containing potato, carrot, and onion. The dish originated in Japan during the Meiji era as a fusion of Western-style breaded cutlet and Indian-influenced curry, and has since become a staple of Japanese home cooking, school canteens, and curry restaurant chains. In Singapore, it is widely available at Japanese food courts, hawker stalls, and fast-casual restaurants. The pork cutlet is coated in panko breadcrumbs and deep-fried until golden and crispy, yielding a crunchy exterior with a juicy, tender interior. The Japanese curry sauce is characteristically mild, slightly sweet, and thickened with a roux, distinguishing it from South Asian or Southeast Asian curries. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, and 231 mg sodium. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 65 (medium), moderated by the fibre and protein content. Clinically, the dish is suitable for most adults in moderate portions but requires caution for patients with hypertension (sodium load), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate GI, deep-fried fat), cardiovascular disease (saturated fat from frying), and rheumatoid arthritis (pork red meat and AGEs from deep-frying are hard disqualifiers). The dish is not halal-certified due to pork content and contains gluten allergens from panko breadcrumbs and curry roux.

confidence_reason: Core nutritional values are sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database (cr_id G00010935) with review_status reviewed and confidence 0.95. The HPB entry is an exact match for this dish as served in Singapore. Saturated fat and fatty acid fractions are not reported by HPB for this entry and are set to -1. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature, clearly documented.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions for pork cutlet curry rice are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for G00010935 as of 2026-04-16. USDA FoodData Central does not have an exact composite match for this dish. These values should be updated when fatty acid data become available from an approved source. 2. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB for this entry. This is noted as potentially anomalous given the pork and egg content of the dish; however, the HPB value is accepted as reported from the approved source. If a future audit finds this to be a data entry error, the value should be corrected. 3. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data beyond those reported by HPB are not available in the HPB Foodo database for this composite dish entry. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on Japanese curry rice with white rice base from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). A direct measurement for pork cutlet curry rice is not available in any approved GI source as of 2026-04-16. 5. Food name trailing period removed: input name was 'Pork Cutlet Curry Rice.' — canonical form is 'Pork Cutlet Curry Rice' without trailing punctuation. 6. This dish contains pork and is not halal. It contains gluten (panko, curry roux) and egg allergens.
